figures.caption: Figure 1: FRESCO (top row) accelerates ESNs by operating natively in the frequency domain while actively bypassing standard FFT bottlenecks. In the frequency domain, dense matrix operations mathematically collapse into fast element-wise products (⊙), lowering end-to-end computational costs compared to dense and sparse standard ESNs (bottom row). Left: The introduced dimensional zero-padding minimizes input embedding overhead. Center: The recurrent state updates are directly executed via element-wise products, and non-linearities Φ̂ are applied in the frequency domain. Right: To match standard readout speeds, the novel packed frequency-domain readout computes yt directly from a memory-contiguous, non-redundant state, entirely avoiding inverse FFTs. Together, these mechanisms yield highly favorable time and memory scaling with increasing input size Nx and reservoir size N (central plots; N = 256Nx; see Appendix A for details). Notation (see Appendix D.3 for a comprehensive list): complex (̂·), real (unaccented), vectors (bold lowercase), matrices (bold uppercase). Bias/leaking terms are omitted.

Figure 2: Performance scaling for Mackey-Glass (left) and NARMA10 (right) for reservoir sizes ∈ {128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048, 4096}. Plots show mean and standard deviation of NRMSE versus end-to-end execution time from input to output for a single time step across 20 random initializations.
Table 2: Forecasting results with selected models and state-of-the-art deep learning baselines taken from [16]. Best and second best are colored red and blue, respectively.
Figure 4: Accuracy, energy, and runtime (training and inference) for long-horizon forecasting, showing FRESCO’s superior efficiency.

Figure 6: Dimensional zero-padding: A 1D input vector x is embedded into a 2D space by padding zeros along the second dimension (columns). The resulting 2D DFT consists simply of repeated columns of the much smaller 1D transform x̂ = F{x}. In practice, neither Xzp nor F{Xzp} need to be created explicitly, since the result x̂ can be broadcast into the matrix representation of the reservoir.

Figure 7: Comparison of minimal computational times (lower is better) for different input embedding methods across increasing input sizes Nx and reservoir sizes N . A fixed scaling ratio of 256 reservoir neurons per input feature is maintained (N = 256Nx). The proposed embedding via dimensional zero-padding exhibits highly efficient scaling behavior, outperforming standard spatial-domain ESN dense layers by orders of magnitude at larger network scales.

Figure 8: Memory layout of the standard RFFT of a 6x6 matrix in a row-major memory format. Real values belonging to index group I0 (c.f. Proposition 5) are shaded gray, while complex elements (without conjugated counterpart) of group I+ are shown on white background. All other colors show redundant complex conjugated pairs (I+, I−) as Hermitian symmetry in the first and the last column. For packed readout, we only need to extract one unique element, say I+ of each color. From the flattened in-memory order one can see that the unique complex elements (I+) are distributed into 3 different non-consecutive segments which does not allow for an efficient bulk memory readout.
Figure 9: Memory layout of the proposed axis-reordered RFFT. See Proposition 5 for a definition of the index groups I0, I+, and I−.

Figure 10: Comparison of minimal computational times for packed frequency-domain readout compared to a conventional ESN readout as baseline, for increasing reservoir size N and output size Ny. The small differences confirm that the packing operation p = P{R̂} is negligible compared to the actual matrix-vector products.

Table 3: Dataset statistics for the time-series classification experiments. # Tr and # Ts denote the number of training and test sequences, respectively. Length is the number of time steps, Input dim. is the number of input channels, and # Classes is the number of target classes.

Table 4: Dataset statistics for long-horizon forecasting with input window length Lin = 96. “Freq.” denotes the sampling frequency, “Ch.” the number of variables, and L the total length of the dataset. “Train”, “Val”, and “Test” indicate the number of time steps in each split. “Train win.”, “Val win.”, and “Test win.” denote the number of sliding-window samples for each horizon.
Table 6: Hyperparameter search ranges for reservoir models in long-horizon forecasting.
Table 7: Full forecasting results parsed from anytime Bayesian optimization logs. The best result is red and the second best result is blue.
